title: Swiggy Delivery Partner LRU Assignment
description:
Swiggy ka dispatch system ek LRU (Least Recently Used) cache use karta hai delivery partner ke recent assignment dhyan rakhne ke liye. Cache me partner ka ID (key) aur unka current zone (value) store hota hai. Jab capacity full ho jaaye aur naya partner add ho, toh sabse purana (least recently used) hata do.

Implement an LRU cache with `get(key)` and `put(key, value)`, both in O(1).

Since the grader runs a flat function, dispatch operations as an array. The driver function `runOps` receives `ops` like `[["LRUCache", capacity], ["put", k, v], ["get", k], ...]`. Constructor pushes `null`; `put` pushes `null`; `get` pushes the value (or `-1` if absent).

examples.explanation: Capacity 2; key 2 evicted after put(3); key 1 evicted after put(4).

hints: Doubly-linked list + hash map combo — DLL maintain order, map gives O(1) node access.
Dummy head/tail sentinels rakh — boundary checks zero ho jaate hain.
Get bhi 'recent' bana deta hai — node ko head ke paas move kar.

title: PhonePe Transaction LFU Cache
description:
PhonePe ka risk-engine LFU (Least Frequently Used) cache use karta hai recent transaction profiles store karne ke liye. Jab capacity full ho aur eviction zaroori ho, sabse kam baar use hua entry hatao; tie ho toh among those sabse purana (LRU within LFU bucket).

Implement `get(key)` and `put(key, value)`, both O(1). Use the operations array — `[["LFUCache", cap], ["put", k, v], ["get", k], ...]`.

examples.explanation: Key 2 evicted (freq 1) on put(3). Then key 1 evicted (freq 2 vs 3 also at 2 but 1 LRU).

hints: Freq → ordered set of keys (insertion-ordered Map works in JS).
Track `minFreq` so eviction is O(1).
On get/put existing, bump freq and move key from old freq bucket to new.

title: Razorpay Min Stack of Fee Adjustments
description:
Razorpay ka fee-pipeline ek stack rakhta hai jisme har push ek fee adjustment hai. Engineering ko kabhi bhi pata chahiye stack me sabse chhota current fee — sab kuch O(1) me. Implement a `MinStack` supporting `push(x)`, `pop()`, `top()`, `getMin()`, all in O(1).

Ops array: `[["MinStack"], ["push", x], ["pop"], ["top"], ["getMin"], ...]`. Constructor and `push`/`pop` push `null`; `top` and `getMin` push the value.
constraints: pop/top/getMin called only on non-empty stack
hints: Auxiliary stack of running mins parallel to main stack.
Push only when new value <= current min; pop in lockstep when popped value === min.
Single stack with [val, min] tuples bhi kaam karta hai.

title: Flipkart Stack via Two Queues
description:
Flipkart ke training quiz me ek classic question — implement a LIFO stack using only two queues (FIFO). Support `push(x)`, `pop()`, `top()`, `empty()`.

Ops array: `[["MyStack"], ["push", x], ["pop"], ["top"], ["empty"]]`. Constructor and `push` push `null`; `pop`/`top` push the value; `empty` pushes a boolean.
constraints: pop/top called only on non-empty stack
hints: Costly push approach — har push pe q1 me daal aur q2 ke saamne rotate kar.
Result: q1's front always the most-recently pushed = stack top.
Single queue bhi kaam karta hai — rotate it after push.

title: Paytm Queue via Two Stacks
description:
Paytm ke onboarding system me FIFO queue chahiye but available data-structure library me sirf stacks hain. Build a `MyQueue` using only two stacks supporting `push(x)`, `pop()`, `peek()`, `empty()` — amortized O(1) for each.

Ops array: `[["MyQueue"], ["push", x], ["pop"], ["peek"], ["empty"]]`.
constraints: pop/peek called only on non-empty queue
hints: Two stacks: `inStack` for pushes, `outStack` for pops.
Move from in→out only when out is empty; preserves FIFO.
Amortized O(1) — each element moves at most once between stacks.

title: Ola HashSet From Scratch
description:
Wait — actually a Medium. Ola ke fraud-detection module me apna hashset chahiye taaki built-in libraries pe dependency na ho. Implement `MyHashSet` with `add(key)`, `remove(key)`, `contains(key)` — all without using language Set/Map for the storage.

Ops array: `[["MyHashSet"], ["add", k], ["remove", k], ["contains", k]]`. Constructor/add/remove push `null`; `contains` pushes a boolean.
hints: Bucket array of size ~1009 (prime). Each bucket is a list (chaining) of keys.
Hash = key % numBuckets.
Linear search inside the bucket — buckets stay short.

title: Myntra Time-Based Price Store
description:
Myntra ka price-history service har SKU ka price timestamp ke saath store karta hai. Pricing analyst ko kabhi bhi pata chahiye: 'is timestamp pe (ya us se pehle ka latest) is SKU ka price kya tha?' Timestamps strictly increasing diye jaate hain per key.

Implement `set(key, value, timestamp)` and `get(key, timestamp)`. `get` returns the value with the largest timestamp `<=` queried timestamp; if none, return empty string `""`.

Ops: `[["TimeMap"], ["set", k, v, ts], ["get", k, ts]]`. Constructor/set push `null`; get pushes the string.
hints: Per-key array of [timestamp, value]; ts is sorted (problem guarantees).
Binary search for largest ts <= query.
Empty string when query ts < earliest stored ts.

title: Koo Twitter-Style News Feed
description:
Koo ka social feed engine — implement `postTweet(userId, tweetId)`, `follow(followerId, followeeId)`, `unfollow(followerId, followeeId)`, `getNewsFeed(userId)`. `getNewsFeed` returns up to 10 most recent tweet IDs from the user themselves and the people they follow, newest first.

Ops: `[["Twitter"], ["postTweet", u, tw], ["follow", f, t], ["unfollow", f, t], ["getNewsFeed", u]]`. Constructor/post/follow/unfollow push `null`; getNewsFeed pushes array.
hints: Per user: list of (timestamp, tweetId).
Per user: set of followee IDs (don't forget self).
Feed: collect from self + followees, sort by ts desc, take 10.

title: CRED Credit Score Snapshot Array
description:
CRED ka credit-score module ek snapshot-able array maintain karta hai. Indexes 0..length-1 sab 0 se start hote hain. Ops: `set(idx, val)`, `snap()` returning the next snap_id (starts 0), `get(idx, snap_id)` returning value at that index when that snapshot was taken.

Ops: `[["SnapshotArray", length], ["set", i, v], ["snap"], ["get", i, snap_id]]`. Constructor/set push `null`; `snap` pushes the new snap_id; `get` pushes the value.
hints: Naive: full copy on snap — O(n) time, O(n*snaps) space. Bahut zyada.
Per-index list of [snap_id, val] writes; binary search on get.
Track current `snap_id` counter — only writes since last snap matter.

title: Razorpay API Hit Counter
description:
Razorpay ka API gateway count karna chahta hai last 300 seconds (5 minutes) me kitne hits aaye. `hit(timestamp)` ek hit log karta hai (timestamps in seconds, monotonically non-decreasing). `getHits(timestamp)` returns hits in the window `(timestamp-300, timestamp]`.

Ops: `[["HitCounter"], ["hit", ts], ["getHits", ts]]`. Constructor/hit push `null`; getHits pushes the count.
hints: Queue of timestamps. On getHits/hit, drop entries with ts <= now-300.
If timestamps cluster heavily, use bucket per second (size 300).
For follow-up: 'what if many hits per second?' — bucketed approach scales.

title: Razorpay Token Bucket Rate Limiter
description:
Razorpay ka API gateway token-bucket rate-limiter use karta hai. Bucket capacity `cap` hai aur har second `refillRate` tokens add hote hain (capped at cap). `allow(timestamp)` ek request: agar token available hai, consume 1 and return `true`; else `false`. Bucket starts full.

Tokens are refilled lazily — between calls ka elapsed time integer-second granularity pe compute karo: `tokens = min(cap, tokens + (now - lastRefill) * refillRate)`. Use floor — partial tokens carry as fractional? **No** — keep tokens as integer count, refill `(now-lastRefill) * refillRate` tokens (timestamps in whole seconds, monotonic).

Ops: `[["RateLimiter", cap, refillRate], ["allow", ts]]`. Constructor pushes `null`; allow pushes boolean.

examples.explanation: Cap 3, refill 1/s. First 3 succeed at t=1. 4th rejected. At t=2 one token refilled, one allow ok, second rejected.

hints: Lazy refill — only update tokens when allow() is called.
tokens = min(cap, tokens + (now - lastRefillTs) * refillRate). Then update lastRefillTs = now.
Bucket starts at cap (full).

title: Flipkart Product Search Autocomplete
description:
Flipkart ka search-bar autocomplete bana — user character-by-character type karta hai (lowercase a-z and `#` to commit). Past committed sentences ka frequency rakho, aur har keystroke pe suggestions return karo: top 3 sentences (by frequency desc, then ASCII asc) jinka prefix abhi-tak typed hai.

Constructor takes initial sentences and counts. `input(c)`: if `c == '#'`, commit current buffer (increment freq, reset) and return `[]`. Else append `c` to buffer and return up to 3 matching sentences.

Ops: `[["AutocompleteSystem", sentences, counts], ["input", c]]`. Constructor pushes `null`; input pushes the suggestions array.

examples.output: [null,["i love you","island","i love leetcode"],["i love you","i love leetcode"],[],[]]

constraints: Sentences contain a-z and space only
input chars are a-z, space, or #
hints: Map<sentence, freq>. Filter by prefix on each input — small data so O(N) scan ok.
Sort filtered by (-freq, sentence asc), take 3.
On '#', merge current buffer into freq map.

tests.label: commits new sentence shows in next prefix

title: Paytm Idempotency Randomized Set
description:
Paytm idempotency-key store me operations chahiye: `insert(val)` → returns `true` if newly added, `remove(val)` → returns `true` if was present, `getRandom()` → returns a uniformly-random present value. All in O(1) average.

For deterministic testing, the grader replaces `Math.random` so `getRandom` returns the **last inserted element still present**. Implement using `Math.random` in your code; the test harness deterministically forces the last index. (The tests below use sequences where this is unambiguous.)

Actually — to make this deterministic for the grader, define `getRandom()` to return the element at index `array.length - 1` (the most recently added still-present element). This sidesteps randomness while still validating O(1) data-structure design.

Ops: `[["RandomizedSet"], ["insert", x], ["remove", x], ["getRandom"]]`. Constructor pushes `null`; insert/remove push boolean; getRandom pushes a number.
constraints: getRandom only called when set non-empty
hints: Array of values + Map<val, idx> for O(1) lookup.
Remove: swap-with-last, pop, update map.
getRandom — return values[values.length - 1] (deterministic per problem note).

title: Delhi Metro Underground Travel System
description:
Delhi Metro ka travel-time system: passengers tap-in at one station and tap-out at another. Track average travel time between station pairs.

- `checkIn(id, station, t)` — passenger `id` enters `station` at time `t`.
- `checkOut(id, station, t)` — same passenger exits at `station` at time `t`. Updates the avg between their checkIn station and this one.
- `getAverageTime(start, end)` — return avg trip time from `start` to `end` (over all completed trips matching that ordered pair).

Return from `getAverageTime` is a number (use floating point — match expected to within problem tolerance, but for grader simplicity all expected values are exact integers in the tests below).

Ops: `[["UndergroundSystem"], ["checkIn", id, st, t], ["checkOut", id, st, t], ["getAverageTime", a, b]]`.
constraints: 1 <= t <= 10^9, t increases per id between in/out
getAverageTime always called for a pair with at least one completed trip
hints: Per-passenger pending check-in: Map<id, [station, time]>.
Per-pair stats: Map<"a→b", [totalTime, count]>.
Average = total / count; problem tests expect integer answers so plain integer division works here.

title: Zomato Logger Rate Limiter
description:
Zomato ka order-event logger rate-limit karta hai duplicate messages ko. `shouldPrintMessage(timestamp, message)` returns `true` if the message can be printed at this timestamp, else `false`. A message is printable only if it hasn't been printed in the last 10 seconds (so if it was printed at time `t`, the next allowed time is `t + 10`).

Additionally, support `purge(timestamp)` which evicts all entries whose last-printed time is `<= timestamp - 10` (memory hygiene). `purge` returns the number of entries evicted.

Ops: `[["Logger"], ["shouldPrintMessage", t, msg], ["purge", t]]`. Constructor pushes `null`; shouldPrintMessage pushes boolean; purge pushes the integer evicted count.
hints: Map<msg, lastPrintedTs>. Allowed if !has(msg) or t - lastTs >= 10.
Purge: iterate map, delete entries where ts <= now - 10.
Update map only on a successful print.
